Doris Wang
UX Design Data Analytics
Case Study · 2025 — Present

Snowplango.Every resort, ranked for you.

A skiing companion app that scores 813 North-American resorts by your riding style — and lets an AI co-pilot plan the trip around it.

Role
Lead UX/UI Designer
Paired with 1 engineer
Platform
iOS · Native
Mobile-first product
Status
Live + Closed Beta
Trail forecasting in test
Focus
Positioning vs. OpenSnow
Design system & AI flow
01 / The Problem

Skiers don't need more weather data.
They need an answer.

Existing tools dump barometric pressure, multi-model forecasts and avalanche risk on you and walk away. The skier still has to translate "8 inches overnight, 18 mph wind, –6°C base" into a real decision: is this resort, this weekend, going to be fun for me?

01

Forecasts treat every skier the same.

A powder hunter and a park rider open the exact same weather screen. Same numbers, same charts — but a 14 inch dump means very different things to each of them.

02

Trip planning lives in twelve tabs.

Users compare resorts across snow apps, IKON/Epic pass coverage, Google Maps, Booking.com, Reddit threads and group chats — then guess. There's no place to hold the whole decision.

03

Conditions change hour-to-hour.

By 11 AM the morning powder is tracked out and the south-facing groomers are ice. A resort-level daily forecast can't see that. Trail-level intelligence can.

02 / Competitive Landscape

OpenSnow gives you the weather.
Snowplango gives you the answer.

OpenSnow is the category leader — and a brilliant meteorology product. But it's built for skiers who already speak fluent forecast. We picked a different lane: turn raw data into a personalised, ranked recommendation that anyone can act on.

Where competitors sit, and the gap we built into.
Competitor
Snowplango
Personalised & Decision-First Generic & Data-First Casual & New Skiers Expert Forecast Users
UntappedA skier-first companion app: tells you where to ski, not how to read a forecast.
Pro toolsPower users who already know what they need.
GeneralistsWeather, but not really for skiing.
Forecast nerdsThe current category leaders live here.
Opportunity
OpenSnow
PEAKS model · daily snow
Snow-Forecast.com

OnTheSnow
Resort directory + reports
Apple Weather
General audience · no ski lens
Resort native apps
One mountain only
Snowplango
Riding-style score · AI planner
Capability OpenSnow Snowplango
Core promise The most accurate mountain weather forecast — multi-model, hourly, 15 days out. The best resort to ski right now, for you — translated from the same kind of data into a single score.
Personalisation Favorites list. Same numbers shown to everyone. Riding-style profile drives a weighted score across Powder · Carving · Visibility · Park.
Decision support Daily Snow editorial articles. User reads & interprets. AI Trip Planner compares resorts, forecasts the best snow days and builds the itinerary in one conversation.
Granularity Resort & coordinate level. Whole-mountain forecasts. Trail-level forecasting by time-of-day — predicting which runs hold powder vs. ice. Closed Beta
Cognitive load High — built for "weather nerds" (their words). Low — one number per dimension, plain-language summary, hourly chip.
Pricing model Subscription · $49.99–$99.99 / yr Free core + premium AI & trail forecasts (in test).
"Powder, carving, visibility & park —
updated hourly, scored for the way you ride."
03 / Logo & Brand Identity

A mark that earns the snow — and the go.

Skiers don't trust generic. The logo had to feel like a tool a serious rider would respect — built from the same geometry as a topo map, not a stock weather icon. Two overlapping peaks, an ice-blue gradient that reads as both cold and premium, and a deep navy ground that lets it sit confidently on a phone home screen next to AllTrails and OpenSnow.

Snowplango
App icon · v1

Two peaks, not one

A single mountain reads as weather app. Twin peaks read as terrain — exactly what a skier is choosing between when they open the app.

Ice gradient over flat fill

The light-to-dark blue gradient mimics actual snow in low-angle morning light. It's the only color move in the brand that's purely emotional — and it earned its keep.

Navy, not black

Black would have been safer and more "premium app." Navy ties the icon back to night-before-the-storm — the actual moment a skier opens this app.

04 / Designed Flow

Splash to scored resort in five screens.

Onboarding and daily use share the same surfaces — there is no tutorial layer separate from the product. Three carousel slides in first-run, then five core screens that carry every session afterwards: Splash, Snow Scores, Find Your Resort, AI Trip Planner, Resort Detail. Each one had to land its own job in under five seconds — read the conditions, find a better mountain, or have a conversation with the data instead of staring at a forecast table.

S0 · WelcomeThe hero moment
S1 · Snow ScoresThe product, in one screen
S3 · AI Trip PlannerConversation, not config
S · SplashLogo intro · 1.2s
S2 · Find Your Resort813-resort database, sorted
05 / App Store & Marketing

A merchandising story that moves units.

App Store screenshots get scrolled past in roughly the time it takes to read this sentence. Every frame has to land one sticky idea — the bold display headline doing 80% of the work, the phone screen proving it, the eyebrow giving it shape. The hero frame opens the story; the next seven build a tight argument for why this app belongs on a skier's phone. Same Navy palette, same Inter × Instrument Serif typography, same scoring colors carried from the product into the merchandising surface.

Hero · App Store frame 01
Frame 01 · The hook

One headline does the selling. The phone proves it.

01
Display sets the tone
Inter 600 at clamp(40, 6vw, 72) — bold enough to read at thumbnail size, restrained enough to feel like product.
02
Italic carries the idea
Instrument Serif italic on the verb — same pairing as the in-app hero. The merchandising surface inherits the system, never invents a new one.
03
Navy floor, not white
App Store thumbnails default to white. We don't. The deep navy reads alpine, reads premium, reads not another weather app.
04
Score colors carry over
The four scoring hues that anchor the product appear in the screenshot below the fold — same tokens, same meaning, no marketing-only palette.
#0A1426 · Navy 0 Inter / Instrument Serif 1320 × 2868 · App Store 6.7"

Web · Landing page covers

Marketing
06 / Design System

Built for an alpine night.
Quiet, cold, with the sky doing the work.

Snowplango opens before sunrise — in lift lines, on freezing fingers, against bright snow glare. The system leans into a deep navy canvas with restrained accents, a slow aurora gradient for atmosphere, and four scoring colors that map one-to-one to the riding-style dimensions everywhere they appear in the product.

Color · Foundation & Score Tokens

Navy 0 · Canvas
#15233F
Navy 2 · Surface
Aurora
Atmosphere
#338CFF
Ice · Powder
#2BD68A

Visibility
Score Tokens — applied
48
Powder
53
Carving
24
Visibility

Park

Each riding dimension owns a hue. The same color tells you "this is your Powder score" on the home card, in the weekly ranking list, in the AI's response and in trail-level forecasts — so the user's eye learns the system once and reads it everywhere.

Color Systems · v1.1.2 — Full Spec

The token system has two color collections that look similar but mean opposite things. Style colors tell you which dimension a number belongs to. Score colors tell you how good that number is. The carving green and the excellent green share a hex by design — keep the tokens separate, never collapse them.

Style Color System Each scoring dimension owns one color — appears on the score, the filter chip, the style card, the run chip.
Score Color System Color is driven by the numeric value, not the style — matches the OpenSnow legend (85+ / 70–84 / 55–69 / <55).
Typography · System
Snowplango
Inter 600
Display · 30/32
Every resort, ranked for you.
Instrument Serif
Headline · 22/26
36°F · 48 Powder
Inter 600
Numeric · 20/24
Powder, carving, visibility & park — updated hourly.
Inter 400
Body · 13/18
Know before you go
JetBrains Mono
Eyebrow · 10/12
Motion · Principles
  • Aurora breathes.
    Background opacity drifts 0.3 → 0.5 → 0.3 over a slow 12s loop — atmosphere, not decoration.
  • Numbers count up.
    Score values animate 0 → target over 0.8s, staggered 80ms across the four dimensions, so the eye reads them in sequence.
  • AI types like a person.
    Tool-use lines stream in italic gray; responses stream token-by-token at 15–25ms — borrowed honestly from ChatGPT & Claude conventions.
  • Reorder is a spring.
    When the user changes filter, resort rows resort with a 0.6s spring (300/30) — never a hard reload.
Iconography · Weather System

Twenty-four conditions, one visual language.

Custom-built rounded glyphs designed for the navy product surface. Each icon shares the same cloud silhouette and subtle dual-tint gradient — sun gets a warm gold accent, snow stays cool blue, thunder picks up a violet edge — so the daily forecast row reads as one system, not an icon-pack mash-up. Bound to Figma Variables; one token swap re-themes the entire weather layer.

07 / What's Next

A product that keeps getting more honest
about what's happening on the mountain.

Currently shipping v1.1.2. The current release answers the resort question. The next two answer the trail question and the trip question — moving Snowplango progressively closer to the only thing the skier actually wants to know: where, on which run, at what time, will the snow be best?

Live
Riding-style scoring & resort ranking Four-dimension score model (Powder · Carving · Visibility · Park) running across 813 North-American resorts, refreshed hourly. Color systems and personalization landed in v1.1.2.
Shipped · v1.1.2
Closed Beta
Trail-level time-of-day forecasting Predict each individual run's condition by morning / midday / afternoon — so the user knows powder windows, ice risk and grooming freshness per trail. Currently in internal test, not yet released.
In test · v1.5
Roadmap
Share Card · Pro tier · contextual paywalls Shareable score cards for social, a Pro tier with unlimited AI summaries & planner usage, and softer monetization moments threaded into the right user journeys.
Designing · v1.7
Roadmap
AI Trip Planner — multi-day itineraries Conversation extends from "what's good this weekend" to lodging, lifts, drive time and après — with the same riding-style profile carried through.
Designing · v2.0

Long-term, the product is positioned for a venture path — building toward the kind of category-defining ski intelligence platform that hasn't existed before. The score model, the trail-level data layer, and the AI conversation surface are all designed to compound: the more skiers use it, the better the ranking becomes, the harder it gets to displace.

08 / My Role

Lead designer,
paired with one engineer.

A two-person team — me on every design surface, one engineer on iOS & the data pipeline. Tight loop, weekly ship cadence, design and code reviewed in the same Figma file.

What I owned

  • Competitive teardowns of OpenSnow, OnTheSnow & Snow-Forecast — and the positioning argument that came out of them.
  • Brand identity from scratch — logo, mark, app icon, navy-to-ice gradient system, typographic lockup.
  • The four-dimension scoring model as a UX construct — naming, color tokens, copy, score-tile component.
  • End-to-end onboarding flow (Splash → Scores → Rankings → AI Planner) including animation specs.
  • Design system foundations: Navy palette, Inter × Instrument Serif type pairing, motion principles, component library.
  • Style + Score color systems (v1.1.2) — token architecture matching the OpenSnow legend, with shared-hex disambiguation rules.
  • Custom weather icon library — eight conditions in one visual language.
  • AI conversation design — tool-use status lines, streaming bubbles, error states, escape hatches.
  • Marketing surfaces: App Store seven-frame story, desktop & mobile landing pages, copywriting.
  • Specs & engineering handoff for v1.0; current design lead on the trail-forecasting beta.

How we worked together

  • Shared Figma file as the single source of truth — engineer commented directly on frames instead of separate tickets.
  • I prototyped score-tile interactions in code so we could agree on motion timing before hand-off, not after.
  • Weekly review of what shipped, what slipped, what the score model got wrong — copy and visual updates driven by real data.
  • Trade-offs negotiated openly: when the trail-forecast model couldn't hit hourly accuracy, we co-designed a "morning / midday / afternoon" UI instead of waiting.
